Implementation of Relationships in Oracle

When the foreign key rule of a relationship is RES or CAS, the relationship is implemented in Oracle by a declarative integrity constraint.

The declarative integrity constraint is as follows:

ALTER TABLE TableName ADD CONSTRAINT ConstraintName FOREIGN KEY(ForeignKeyFields) REFERENCES TableName (ReferencedKeyFields)] [ON DELETE CASCADE]

When the foreign key rule of a relationship is NUL, the relationship is implemented by a set of triggers in the following table:

Table 1. Triggers used to Implement Relationships
Triggers on Purpose
UPDATE on the one table. Disallow update of referenced key.
DELETE on the one table. Nullify referencing keys.
INSERT or UPDATE on the many table. Disallow unmatched foreign key.

Disabling Foreign Key Rules

By default, the Uniface CAS and NUL foreign key rules are enabled when using Oracle. When using Uniface, it is recommended that you do not change this default in a development environment, otherwise there is an overhead of the data conversion procedure.

If you decide to implement the relationships in Oracle, you must set the disable foreign key rules connector option. For more information, see disable foreign key rules.

Limitations

The following limitations apply when using the referential integrity enhancements with Oracle:

  • It is not feasible to create tables on the fly with referential integrity support.

There are some limitations on the use of triggers in Oracle:

  • The trigger for the nullify action is not valid when it attempts to nullify a foreign key in a mutating or constraining table. See your Oracle documentation for more information on mutating and constraining tables.
  • If any of the relationships have the NUL foreign key rule, the Oracle server must have the Procedural Option because these relationships are implemented by database triggers.
  • If any of the relationships have the NUL foreign key rule, the CREATE TRIGGER privilege is required to be able to create the relationships. The CREATE TRIGGER privilege should be given to the UNIFACE_DEVELOPER role.
  • Foreign key fields cannot have the Long, Long Raw, BLOB, or CLOB storage format.
  • When databases have been migrated from earlier ORA connector versions using the backward compatibility method rather than the conversion method, problems can occur when creating the relationships. This is because earlier ORA connector versions created a Unique Index with Not Null constraints for the primary key and candidate key, whereas relationships can only be defined on an Oracle Primary Key or a Unique Key. The generic database conversion procedure should be used to export, re-create and import data, or tables should be modified manually to create the Primary Key and Unique Key constraints.
